speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor is Ruth Oppenheimer, and the date is November 18, 1995. Ruth, we left off on the last tape with you discussing the last visit to your grandparents before you left for Holland. Your grandfather took you out in the back yard to the apple tree, and then you started to talk about your grandmother. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandmother said, I'm not going to the railroad station with you, I can't walk that far. And of course we didn't have a car. So we said goodbye. We knew we wouldn't see her again. We were going to America, and she is 86 years old. And what's going to happen? But we sort of felt secure about her being in her house. It was the house they had lived in for a long, long time. And we didn't realize that that was not going to happen. My grandfather took us to the railroad station. He wore a cap with a visor. And he had grown a little, tiny beard that he kept short. And the train was late. And we stood there, and we said goodbye. And at that point, I felt so horrible deep down inside that I was hoping the train would come already. It was so heart wrenching to leave these two old people there by themselves. The other son had left with his family. They went to Argentina. And they had nobody left anymore. Nobody. They lived in this little city of 5,000, and most of the Jews had either left or been killed. And he stood there, and the tears were rolling into his beard. I don't believe that anybody should go through this. When you were picked up and shipped away, people tell me that you didn't realize what the end was going to be. There, we only knew that we were never going to see him. That whatever help he might need, we couldn't give him. We were too far away. That this was not the end that he deserved. Then the train finally came, and we hugged and kissed once more. And we went on the train. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Shortly after we left, the people who now lived in the house, threw them out of the house. And there was a Jewish community in Cologne. They were very close to Cologne. And the Jewish community in Cologne took them into a home where they were living. And there, they were really better off than in Bedburg, where they were living, because they had back up support. And there were some people who took care of them, not realizing that one day, everybody got picked up out of that home and got shipped to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in America already, by then. And we wrote them, and there was no answer. And we wrote them again, and there was no answer. Until finally, we got a letter saying that they were-- very short letter-- in Cologne, in this old age home. And then the Jewish community in Cologne notified us that they were picked up. That everybody, all the old people in that home, were picked up, put into a cattle car. And my cousins, who had fled to Argentina, got the notice because they inquired. And the Germans-- they kept such damn good records-- said that they went into the cattle car, but they never got out. When they opened the car in Theresienstadt, they must have been dead. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At least they didn't have to go into the camp. I don't know if that's a consolation or not. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you continue your journey after you left them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left-- we got into Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any trouble traveling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. We were on the train in Germany. The lights were out. And all of a sudden, the train stopped, and that was the border. And I said, mom, I only have $2.30 left. I'm going to give it to a charity box. And I did. I said, let my last thing in Germany be something of value. And I found another mark in my pocketbook, and there was an old lady selling flowers. And for the last mark I had, I bought flowers for my mother. Came back on the train. You had to buy coffee. You couldn't get anything on the train. And instead of that, I bought flowers for her. I said, I don't know when I'll be able to get you your Friday night flowers. And we both sat there and cried.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we got into Amsterdam. No. At the border, they pulled us out of the train. And how much money do you have? We said, 15 marks. And what do you have in the suitcase? And they threw everything out of the suitcase. It was only a little suitcase.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they kept us there until the train left. You have to wait for the next train. And they had expected us. We wrote to Holland, to my mother's cousin, that we were coming on such and such a train. So of course, we weren't on that train. When the next train came, we got on that. And all of a sudden, the lights went on on the train. We had crossed into the Netherlands, where there was no black out. And my mother said, now you can see how I'm crying. I said, well, there are two kinds of tears. This must be joy, because you're going to see your husband again after three and a half years. And he's alive, and we're alive. And we're going to stay together. And we got to the railroad station. Of course, there was nobody there to expect us, because they were there at the earlier train. So we went to this place that helps-- Travel Aid, or whatever it's called. And she called up my cousin, my mother's cousin, and he said, put them into a taxi cab. I said, we can't go in a taxicab. We have 15 marks. That's all we have. That's what we're going to America with. And he told them, put them in a cab. I'll pay for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went into a cab. And can you imagine? We went into a cab. We drove from the railroad station to this man's house, whom I had never seen, and my mother hadn't seen since she was four years old. This stranger was taking us in? We got to the house, and there my mother and father-- they had invited my father to be there. The reunion was wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they set us down to dinner. Food we hadn't seen in years. Brown gravy, and white bread, and butter, and God knows what.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this now? 1940?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is still 1939. What time of the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 1939. Yeah. We were there 11-- could've been the end of November. I don't know exactly. But we were there 11 weeks. And then my mother's cousin, who was just fantastic-- and was very wealthy, but sometimes wealthy people don't help-- rented a room for my mother and father, so that they had their privacy. And I stayed with them. And the first thing he did was give us money so we had spending money. And it was bitter cold, and wherever I wanted to go, I walked. Because I said, I need that money when I go to America. I can walk here. And then my father had to go back to the internment camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What camp was he in?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in Hoek van Holland. There were all these Jewish refugees there. And then they invited us to come up and visit, which we did. We had to take a train to go there. And there I met a Dutch lieutenant. And the Dutch lieutenant fell in love with me. And I liked him-- he was very nice-- but he was not a Jew. And after I got out of Germany, I wasn't going to have anything to do with anybody that wasn't a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he took me to a coffee house, and we went dancing, and we had a good time. And my father just kept shaking his head. How could you go out with him? I said, Daddy, I'm 19 years old, and I haven't had any of this. It's been tough. Well, he said to me, the lieutenant, marry me, and I'll take you to India. I said, who in the hell wants to go to India? So we left to go and apply for our visa to go to the United States. We had our passport, we had our affidavits, and we had our immigration number. 15,427. I guess it was 22 to 27. So this lady of the Jewish community in Rotterdam made an appointment for us to see the American consul. And we took a train to Rotterdam. The cousin paid for all that. And she took us to the American consul. And we gave them our passports, and we gave them our affidavits.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, that's all very good and very nice, but your number is 15,427. And you can't go, because your number is not up. We are up to number 7,000. I said, we can't sit here for seven years. We don't get a work permit. We don't want to stay here. Hitler's going to come here. No, Hitler's not going to come here. So he said, come back tomorrow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the woman put us up for the night amongst white sheets, and she fed us this delicious Dutch food. And we had big pieces of white soap. I said this is what life is. It's life. And the next day, we came to the American consul, and there were our three passports open. And he went--

speaker: interviewee
text: [STAMP] [STAMP] [STAMP] And he said, have a good trip. For years, I didn't know the man's name until I had the idea to look up in the passport. He had to sign it. But then it was too late. He wasn't the consul anymore. I couldn't find him to thank him. She took us back, this lady, to Amsterdam. And then we went to the HIAS, and they would make arrangements for us to get a ship to go to America. And it took until January the 3rd for us to get a ship. New Year's Eve, we begged our cousins, take some of your money, go to Switzerland, go to America, visit. Leave this, go away. No, Hitler is not coming. We have nothing to worry about it. We left on the 3rd of January. They put us on the train. They sealed it. And we went to Antwerp to get on the ship, the Westernland. 10,000 ton ship. This big. And the minute my mother stepped on that boat, she got seasick. And we started out our trip through the Channel, the English Channel. And there, already, we saw the ships that were torpedoed by the Nazis. They were lying in the Channel. We were five days out when one morning, I said to my father, the sun is on the wrong side of the ship. We're going back. He said, you're crazy. No, I'm not crazy. The sun was on the wrong side of the ship. It took three hours, at least, to find out that the purser of the ship had jumped overboard to commit suicide. And by law, they have to circle for five to six hours trying to find him. Now this is in January-- the sea is this high. They didn't find him. But all the refugees on that boat went crazy, because we thought we were going back. The only one that didn't know was my mother, because she was in the cabin, seasick.

speaker: interviewer
text: We got to New York two weeks later. What date was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: February the 3rd, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just backtrack one minute. I wanted to ask two questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your father was interned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So prior to the occupation of the Germans in Holland, when he arrived there, the Dutch interred him in camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. The Dutch first got them rooms, and they put two in a room. And they set them up like that. But by the time he was there maybe six months, there were so many of them that they couldn't take care of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took these tremendous barracks that they had empty in Hoek van Holland, and they put them all in there. And it was just a big open barrack. So they got together, and they asked, could we have some wood to build cubicles?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they did. The people that were interred there did it themselves. They built little rooms with doors so that-- they always put two in the room, and they had privacy. And they lived like human beings.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were treated well even though they were interned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. As I said, they took them to different places, and they fed them well. The ones who didn't have clothes got clothes. Because most of them just escaped with the clothes on their back.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned also that your father was one of the organizers of an underground movement. Could you just tell me a little bit more about that? What his activities were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. While they were in that camp in Hoek van Holland, and while we were there visiting-- that was later-- the German planes came overhead already. But when they were interred in that camp, he always kept saying, the Germans are coming. You know, just like the Maginot Line did not hold anybody. And they got together, and they formed a committee. And they asked for shovels and wheelbarrows, and they started digging ditches around the camp. They said, if they come here, we want to be able to defend ourselves. They didn't give them any weapons. All they had was their shovels, and axes, and saws that they used to build these little rooms. But they gave them trucks, eventually, the Dutch government. And this Queen Wilhelmina, she left. She went to England. And her children went to England. They were saved. But my cousin, they were all interned. And the son didn't come back. The other three came back, which was unusual.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were trying to help organize the Dutch people, but most of them didn't want to hear anything. Because they said, it's not going to happen. Just like with Anne Frank later on. They said, only the Jews. Nothing's going to happen to us. But how many millions of the Dutch got killed? And the Dutch were very good to the Jews, very good. So you arrived in America in 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: '40, On the 3rd of February.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you briefly describe your life in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. From Hoboken, they took us to the Bronx. The cousins were all there to greet us. And in the Bronx, one cousin had cooked dinner. And all the Jews from the neighborhood, the ones that came in 1910 and 1920 from all over the world who were now living in the Bronx, they walked in while we were eating this good noodle soup. And they said, so, New, how do you like America? Of course, we liked it fine. We've only been here a half an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father immediately got in touch with his friend in Baltimore. Then the friend said, it's about time you're here. I've been telling you for seven years. So he said, when are you coming? So with 15 marks, that's $3 we each had, we decided that he was the one to go to Baltimore, and got him a train ticket. And he got his suitcase, and he went to Baltimore, and he met his friend again after I don't know how many years. And the friend put him up in a room and said, now what do you want to do? So he decided he wanted to go into the automobile business. So what was the next thing he could do? He became a body and fender man. He worked. And he said, as soon as I have enough saved, I'm going to tell my wife and my daughter to come.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't sit still in New York. I got a job as a housemaid. And for four weeks I worked, and with that money that I made in the four weeks, we went to Baltimore. And we got to Baltimore, and we wrote a letter to my cousin-- to my mother's cousin in Holland-- that we're there, and we now have an address. And of course, we thanked him very much for all he did for us. And in the next mail, we had a check from him for $450. $150 each. And he said, that should help you in your beginning. And we wrote him back and we said, please, leave immediately. It wasn't time. Hitler had marched into Holland. Well, the three of us were in Baltimore. And this Mr. Kern, Robert Kern, a friend of my dad's, got him a job. And got me a job as a second floor maid, and my mother, in the same house, a cook. So we each made $6 or $8 a week. It was wonderful. We were in America. We were in a beautiful house with very wealthy people. They gave us our affidavit. And life was great. Except my mother and I lived in one end of the city and my father lived in the other, in a furnished room. And he said, this has got to stop. Because we lived apart for 3 1/2 years, and I want to live with my wife, at least.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day the woman of the house said, you don't cook like we cook here in America. And I would like for you to leave and get another job. But I don't want you to cook for us anymore. Your daughter can stay as a second floor maid.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so my mother and father rented a big old house, and they made a boarding house out of it. And it had, I think, 11 rooms, 12 rooms, and an apartment upstairs. So they rented the apartment upstairs to a couple who had come with us on the boat. And the other rooms were then rented to shipyard workers. And my mother cooked for the shipyard workers and made sandwiches for them to take along. And my father was doing his body and fender job. And I was still second floor maid. Until I became ill. I had to have an operation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I came back from the hospital, I was sitting in front of the house where my parents had rented this house, with one of the women from the house. And the sun was shining. It was beautiful. And two men walked by-- three men. And they stopped dead in their tracks and came backwards. And one of them said to me, huh, how long have you been here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we started talking. In those days, you could still talk to strangers. And besides, I had a chaperone. And I liked him the minute I saw him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother had just come outside. And so he said, how long have you been here? And I said, a few months. And he had immigrated already since 1933. He would have come anyway. And so my mother invited him, the others too, also, to come that evening.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because this older house that they rented had a piano, and Friday nights, my father would play the piano. And everybody from the house would come, and we would sing songs and entertain ourselves. And it was just wonderful. We were in America, and we were alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came. And he asked me, would I like to go swimming the next day? I said, no, I'm still recuperating. He says, all right. I'll pick you up in a cab. And in those days, you know, you didn't get cabs so fast. It turned out that he was a linoleum salesman in a company.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on Saturday, we went to the swimming pool, and we sat in the sun. And he took me and brought me in a cab. And on Sunday, he came and he took me to the shore, where all the refugees had gotten together to rent this shore. And on Monday, he came and just sat. And on Tuesday, he came. For two weeks. Every single day. And I said, tomorrow I'm going to Camp Louise to recuperate. He followed me to Camp Louise and proposed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is William Oppenheimer.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: 55 Years ago. 1940. What an eventful year that was. I got to America, got a husband. I said, now all I've got to do is learn to speak English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a daughter. She was born in 1945. And she's adopted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Shirley Louise, after my grandmother, Foutch. And her husband's name is William.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what career did you eventually take up in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I left being a second floor maid, I became a worker in a pajama factory. I mean, after all, I learned how to make coats and suits. But in the pajama factory, all I did was sew pockets on pajamas by the hundreds. You got piecework. It was very hard work. And after we were married, I changed. I went to another factory, and I sewed in sleeves in blouses. Until one day, I said, this is too much, and I became a sales lady in a store. I sold dresses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there I graduated into being a handbag buyer. And when did you come to Phoenix?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this daughter of ours developed asthma. And for years and years, we tried to find a cure for that. But it wasn't that bad. She had it once in a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as she grew older, she also got married. And it was a beautiful Jewish wedding to a nice Jewish boy. And in those days, you know, some girls married not only out of faith, but out of color. So this guy, he was Jewish, he was white, and he lived in Baltimore. What more could we ask for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had just a wonderful time. It was the year we were married 25 years. We had a party. We had an engagement party. And we had the greatest wedding ever, with two rabbis and a cantor. But it didn't last. She had two sons. Wonderful boys who we adore. And after 22 years of marriage, she divorced her first husband. But she's married again, out of faith, to a man 14 years her junior-- he's 14 years younger-- whom we adore, who is wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you all came to Phoenix together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. She came first, with her first husband. And a year later, we decided, what are we doing in Baltimore when they are here? We want to be a family. So we came to Phoenix.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think I'd like you to bring in your family now, So we can introduce them.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I don't think the young ones are here.
